content: Hello, I am doing a research project. All the details you will need are in an markdown file in  the folder. Construct all architecture and code and give me instructions on what to run and what to do the finish the research project. Ask me if you have any questions especially major decisions that can heavily alter the project's output/scope so you do not mess anything up

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Estimation-in-the-loop J2 formation-keeping: a fast-track arXiv proposal
2	
3	**A Resonance Foundation computational research proposal — target: arXiv preprint in ≤ 8 weeks**
4	
5	This proposal defines a compact, publication-ready research program that extends the single most-cited prior work on relative-navigation-versus-control-cost tradeoffs in formation flying — Koenig & D'Amico (JGCD 2018) — into a modern **Pareto characterization of J2 formation-keeping ΔV as a function of relative-navigation accuracy, across multiple controllers and multiple relative-motion state transition matrices (STMs)**. The angle is deliberately narrow: we do not attempt to advance the physics of J2-perturbed relative motion, nor invent a new controller, nor propose new nav hardware. We hold each of those axes fixed to the best published models and instead sweep the estimation-in-the-loop coupling — the axis where the field's existing analyses stop early. The output is a small, defensible, mission-relevant preprint with a full open-source Python reference implementation. The user's estimation / error-state Kalman filter background is the natural home for this contribution.
6	
7	The remainder of this document contains: (§1) the physics and mathematics foundations at the level of an arXiv methods section; (§2) the literature landscape with an explicit novelty-gap map; (§3) the recommended Python toolchain with tradeoffs; (§4) a fast, phase-by-phase execution plan with weekly milestones; (§5) the paper's structural blueprint including title options, target figures, and the quantitative results that will make the preprint compelling.
8	
9	---
10	
11	## 1. Physics and mathematics foundations
12	
13	### 1.1 The rotating Hill/LVLH frame and Clohessy–Wiltshire equations
14	
15	Consider a **chief** satellite on a reference orbit and a nearby **deputy**. Attach a right-handed rotating frame at the chief with **x̂ radial** (nadir-to-zenith), **ẑ along the orbit angular-momentum vector**, and **ŷ = ẑ × x̂ completing the frame** (roughly along-track for near-circular orbits). This is the local-vertical-local-horizontal (LVLH) or Hill frame. Let **ρ = [x, y, z]ᵀ** be the deputy position expressed in this rotating frame relative to the chief.
16	
17	For a **circular chief orbit of mean motion n = √(μ/a³)** in a spherically symmetric gravity field, expanding the two-body relative acceleration to first order in ‖ρ‖/a yields the Clohessy–Wiltshire (CW) or Hill equations:
18	
19	- ẍ − 2nẏ − 3n²x = uₓ
20	- ÿ + 2nẋ = u_y
21	- z̈ + n²z = u_z
22	
23	where **u** is the control acceleration in the LVLH frame. The out-of-plane channel decouples into a simple harmonic oscillator at frequency n; the in-plane channels couple through Coriolis terms. Writing the state as **X = [x, y, z, ẋ, ẏ, ż]ᵀ**, the closed-form ballistic solution admits an analytic 6×6 state transition matrix **Φ_CW(t)** with entries composed of sin(nt), cos(nt), nt, and constants. The classic sub-blocks read
24	
25	Φ_CW(t) = ⎡ 4−3c   0   0     s/n         2(1−c)/n   0        ⎤
26	          ⎢ 6(s−nt) 1   0    −2(1−c)/n   (4s−3nt)/n 0        ⎥
27	          ⎢ 0      0   c     0            0          s/n      ⎥
28	          ⎢ 3n·s   0   0    c             2s          0        ⎥
29	          ⎢ 6n(c−1)0   0    −2s           4c−3        0        ⎥
30	          ⎣ 0      0  −n·s   0            0           c        ⎦
31	
32	with s = sin(nt), c = cos(nt). Two features matter for formation flying: **(i)** the along-track secular term 6(s−nt) makes any nonzero radial-velocity offset accumulate a linearly growing along-track drift, so a "no-drift" initial condition requires ẏ₀ = −2n x₀ (the CW energy-matching condition), and **(ii)** the closed relative orbits are elliptical in the x-y plane with 2:1 axis ratio when the drift condition holds. Popular design targets — the projected circular orbit (PCO), the general elliptical relative orbit (GERO), and pure along-track leader–follower — are all parameterized inside this family. CW is the *plant model of last resort*: exact for a circular chief in point-mass gravity, wrong (in a bounded way) for anything else.
33	
34	### 1.2 J2 physics and secular element rates
35	
36	Earth is oblate. The dominant non-spherical gravity term is the **J2 zonal harmonic**, with J2 ≈ 1.0826 × 10⁻³. The J2 disturbing potential
37	
38	R_J2 = (μ/r) · J2 · (R_E/r)² · ½ · (3 sin²φ − 1)
39	
40	introduces perturbing accelerations that, when averaged over an orbit (Brouwer / Kozai secular theory), produce **linear secular drifts** in three orbital elements while leaving a, e, i unchanged in mean:
41	
42	- Ω̇ = − (3/2) n J2 (R_E/p)² cos i
43	- ω̇ = + (3/4) n J2 (R_E/p)² (5 cos²i − 1)
44	- Ṁ_secular_perturbation = + (3/4) n J2 (R_E/p)² √(1−e²) (3 cos²i − 1)
45	
46	with p = a(1−e²) the semi-latus rectum. Right-ascension of the ascending node (RAAN) regresses in prograde orbits, hence **nodal precession**; argument-of-perigee rotation vanishes at the critical inclination i ≈ 63.4°; mean-motion perturbation shifts the effective orbital period. **Two spacecraft with even tiny differences in a, e, or i see differential secular rates in Ω, ω, M**, and those differentials drive the relative orbit to drift and eventually to disperse. This is the entire operational problem: **the differential J2 drift is what the formation-keeping controller must expend ΔV to suppress**.
47	
48	Linearizing the secular rates in the orbital-element differences δœ = [δa, δe, δi, δΩ, δω, δM]ᵀ (or, better, in an appropriate quasi-nonsingular / equinoctial variant to avoid the e→0 and i→0 singularities) gives the **differential secular rate matrix** whose entries directly parameterize how each element mismatch degrades formation geometry per orbit.
49	
50	### 1.3 Schweighart–Sedwick J2-modified Hill equations
51	
52	The most compact way to graft J2 onto CW while keeping a linear-time-invariant (LTI) plant is the **Schweighart–Sedwick (S-S) 2002 model**. It re-derives the LVLH relative dynamics with the J2 mean-motion, nodal, and argument-of-perigee secular effects folded into the frequency of the linearized system. The resulting equations retain the CW form but with **modified frequencies**:
53	
54	- ẍ − 2 n c ẏ − (5 c² − 2) n² x = uₓ + (a couple of secular drift terms depending on i and reference latitude)
55	- ÿ + 2 n c ẋ = u_y
56	- z̈ + q² z = 2 l q cos(qt + φ) + u_z
57	
58	where c and q are inclination- and altitude-dependent J2-corrected coefficients, and l, φ absorb the initial-condition offsets induced by J2. Concretely, c = √(1 + s) and q = n c k, where s and k are compact algebraic combinations of J2, R_E/a, and inclination. Under S-S, the out-of-plane channel picks up a J2 forced-oscillation term that produces the cross-track drift observed in real LEO formations, and the in-plane channels have modified natural frequencies. Its assumptions and validity range: **circular chief**, **small formation size** (‖ρ‖/a ≪ 1), **secular effects only** (no long-period or short-period J2), **no drag/SRP**. In practice S-S is very good to ~10⁴ orbital periods for LEO formations up to ~10 km at typical Sun-synchronous inclinations, and it is the model most commonly used in LQR/MPC-based J2 formation controller design because it is LTI.
59	
60	### 1.4 Differential orbital elements and the Gim–Alfriend state transition matrix
61	
62	The **differential-orbital-element (δœ)** approach parameterizes the relative motion by the mean-element differences rather than by Cartesian coordinates. Advantages: (i) the secular J2 drift enters as a diagonal (or nearly diagonal) linear map on δœ, so its physics is transparent; (ii) it handles eccentric chiefs naturally (unlike S-S); (iii) it isolates non-drifting geometric offsets — those with δa = 0 and (in mean elements) matching secular rates — from drifting ones.
63	
64	The **Gim–Alfriend state transition matrix (GA-STM)** (Gim & Alfriend, JGCD 2003; equinoctial variant 2005) propagates the relative state via three composed maps:
65	
66	Φ_GA(t; t₀) = A(t) · Σ(t, t₀) · B(t₀)
67	
68	where **B(t₀)** maps the Cartesian LVLH state at t₀ to mean-element differences at t₀ through the analytic Brouwer mean-to-osculating transformation; **Σ(t, t₀)** propagates mean-element differences forward under the mean secular J2 rates plus first-order secular corrections; and **A(t)** maps the propagated mean-element differences back to Cartesian LVLH at t, again through the osculating map. The GA-STM includes short-period and long-period J2 effects to first order and works for arbitrarily eccentric chief. It is more accurate than S-S (particularly for eccentric chiefs and for long propagation) at the cost of a more elaborate implementation and of no longer being LTI.
69	
70	### 1.5 Modern relative-orbital-element (ROE) STMs — Koenig, Guffanti, D'Amico 2017
71	
72	The **quasi-nonsingular relative orbital elements** (ROE), introduced by D'Amico for the E/I-vector separation formulation, are
73	
74	δœ_qns = [ δa, δλ, δeₓ, δe_y, δiₓ, δi_y ]ᵀ
75	
76	where δa = (a_d − a_c)/a_c (dimensionless semi-major-axis difference), δλ is the mean-argument-of-latitude difference (dimensionless in-plane offset), (δeₓ, δe_y) is the relative eccentricity vector, and (δiₓ, δi_y) is the relative inclination vector. Under J2 secular dynamics, five of these six coordinates evolve linearly at analytically known rates in a, e, i of the chief, and one (δa) is invariant. Koenig, Guffanti & D'Amico (JGCD 2017, DOI 10.2514/1.G002409) give a unified derivation of **first-order secular J2 (and optionally differential-drag) ROE STMs** for singular, quasi-nonsingular, and nonsingular ROE, for arbitrary eccentricity. These are the state-of-the-art plant models. The Guffanti & D'Amico (JGCD 2019) SRP extension adds solar-radiation-pressure secular terms in the same formalism.
77	
78	For this project the **KGD-STM in quasi-nonsingular ROE** is the primary analytic plant, with **S-S** as an LTI convenience for LQR and **GA-STM** as a fidelity cross-check. All three will be implemented and compared.
79	
80	### 1.6 Formation geometry and J2-invariant conditions
81	
82	For a circular chief in two-body gravity, the **CW no-drift condition** ẏ₀ + 2n x₀ = 0 eliminates the secular along-track term. The resulting closed relative orbit is a 2:1 in-plane ellipse; adding out-of-plane sinusoidal motion at frequency n and 90° phase produces the **projected circular orbit (PCO)** — a circle when projected on the y-z plane — used by many science missions requiring uniform azimuthal coverage. General elliptical relative orbits (GERO) generalize this by relaxing the phase constraint.
83	
84	Under J2, the CW no-drift condition is insufficient because the differential J2 secular rates re-inject drift through the δœ channels. **Schaub & Alfriend (CMDA 2001) J2-invariant relative orbits** require matching *mean* secular rates between the two spacecraft. Matching Ω̇ and Ṁ + ω̇ (the mean-argument-of-latitude rate) at fixed mean a, e, i is possible only through specific relations between δa, δe, δi — and even then only for zeroth-order secular J2, not for the higher-order or drag terms. In quasi-nonsingular ROE the J2-invariant condition is compact: δa is exactly conserved by J2 secular dynamics; the passive-safety-friendly E/I-vector separation formulation (D'Amico & Montenbruck, JGCD 2006) chooses phases in (δeₓ, δe_y, δiₓ, δi_y) to guarantee minimum-distance safety while accepting a small in-track drift proportional to δa.
85	
86	### 1.7 Control formulations
87	
88	Given a linear (or linearized) relative-motion plant **Ẋ = A(t) X + B u**, three controller families are used:
89	
90	**Continuous linear-quadratic regulator (LQR)** on S-S or KGD-STM plants. Minimize J = ∫(XᵀQX + uᵀRu) dt with steady-state Riccati solution u = −K X, K = R⁻¹BᵀP, AᵀP + PA − PBR⁻¹BᵀP + Q = 0. Straightforward to compute in `python-control`. Assumes continuous low-thrust actuation.
91	
92	**Impulsive maneuver planning** — treat control as a sequence of Δv-vectors at specific true-anomaly locations. Vaddi–Alfriend–Vadali (JGCD 2005), Gaias & D'Amico (JGCD 2015), and especially **Chernick & D'Amico (JGCD 2018, 2021)** closed-form reachable-set solutions give fuel-optimal impulsive control for near-circular J2 formations. The reachable-set formulation solves min ‖Δv‖₁ subject to δœ(t_f) = δœ_target via a compact primer-vector-like analysis.
93	
94	**Continuous low-thrust Lyapunov feedback** on δœ — Steindorf et al. (AAS 2017), Di Mauro et al. (JGCD 2019). Weighted feedback on the six ROE components with saturation.
95	
96	**Delta-V cost metric.** For a maintenance controller, the annualized ΔV cost ΔV/yr is the standard scalar. Formation-keeping tightness is measured by the RMS or 99th-percentile relative-position error over the campaign, computed in LVLH.
97	
98	### 1.8 Estimation and the closed-loop nav-plus-control problem
99	
100	A real formation controller does not know the deputy's relative state; it receives an **estimate X̂ from a relative navigation filter**, and applies u = −K X̂. This introduces a coupling between estimator covariance and closed-loop performance whose analysis is the paper's central object.
101	
102	**Measurement models we will study.**
103	
104	1. **Carrier-phase differential GPS (CDGPS)**: differential double-difference carrier-phase and pseudorange from a common GNSS constellation, providing centimeter-to-decimeter relative-position and mm/s relative-velocity. Standard in LEO; flight-demonstrated on PRISMA, TanDEM-X, GRACE-FO. Modeled as direct (possibly biased) measurements of relative position in Earth-fixed or LVLH frame with tunable 1σ range in {1 cm, 10 cm, 1 m} for the sweep.
105	2. **Inter-satellite RF range and range-rate**: scalar range (and possibly range-rate via Doppler), typical accuracy 10 cm–10 m depending on link budget. Weakly observable in one direction; the classic angles-plus-range architecture recovers observability.
106	3. **Angles-only optical relative navigation**: bearing measurements from an on-board camera. The classical **Woffinden dilemma** — angles alone are ambiguous under linear rendezvous dynamics — is broken by J2 non-linearity or by observability-improving maneuvers. Sullivan & D'Amico (JGCD 2017) show that formulating the filter directly in ROE recovers observability more efficiently than a Cartesian formulation.
107	
108	**Error-state Kalman filter (ES-KF)**. Rather than filtering the total state (which mixes large deterministic secular motion with small stochastic errors), we filter the **error state** δX = X − X̂_nominal about a reference trajectory propagated by the STM. This is the standard approach in inertial navigation and in D'Amico-group ROE navigation. The ES-KF equations follow the usual EKF prediction/update, with the STM Φ(t_{k+1}, t_k) as the discrete linear propagation and Jacobians H_k for each measurement type:
109	
110	- Prediction: δX̂_{k+1|k} = Φ δX̂_{k|k};  P_{k+1|k} = Φ P_{k|k} Φᵀ + Q_k
111	- Update: K = P Hᵀ (H P Hᵀ + R)⁻¹;  δX̂ ← δX̂ + K (z − H δX̂);  P ← (I − K H) P (Joseph form for numerical robustness).
112	
113	The unscented variant (UKF, Merwe sigma points) is a drop-in for the strongly nonlinear cases (angles-only, cross-track initialization) and will serve as an ablation to check that our EKF conclusions are not artifacts of Jacobian linearization.
114	
115	**Certainty equivalence and the separation principle.** For a purely linear-Gaussian LQG problem, the **separation principle** justifies designing controller and estimator independently and using **u = −K X̂** — the optimal LQG law. For our problem the separation principle is *approximately* valid but not exactly: J2 formation dynamics are linearizable but not exactly linear, actuator saturation may bite when nav error is large, and impulsive controllers do not fit the LQG framework at all. The paper's core empirical claim is that **certainty equivalence is a good baseline for tight-nav regimes but breaks down predictably as nav-error 1σ crosses a controller-dependent threshold, producing a nontrivial and previously uncharacterized Pareto surface between ΔV, control precision, and nav accuracy under J2.**
116	
117	**Process-noise modeling.** Q must account for unmodeled forces (higher zonal harmonics, drag, SRP, third-body) that the STM does not include. We will calibrate Q against the residual between truth propagator and S-S/KGD-STM propagation. **Measurement-noise modeling** R follows published sensor performance: 1–10 cm for CDGPS, 0.1–10 m for RF range, 10–100 arcsec for angles-only cameras, all as tunable inputs to the sweep.
118	
119	---
120	
121	## 2. Literature landscape and the novelty argument
122	
123	### 2.1 Foundational works
124	
125	The field organizes around a compact set of anchor papers. **Clohessy & Wiltshire (JAS 1960)** is the original CW derivation for rendezvous. **Tschauner & Hempel (Astronaut. Acta 1965)** extends to elliptic chiefs. **Schweighart & Sedwick (JGCD 2002)** grafts J2 secular effects onto CW with an LTI plant. **Gim & Alfriend (JGCD 2003; CMDA 2005 equinoctial variant)** give the first-order-J2 geometric STM for arbitrary eccentricity. **Alfriend, Vadali, Gurfil, How & Breger (Elsevier 2010)** is the field textbook. **Schaub & Junkins** (AIAA Education Series, 4th ed. 2018) introduces the orbital-element-difference framework used by the Texas A&M and CU-Boulder schools. **Schaub & Alfriend (CMDA 2001)** define J2-invariant relative orbits. **D'Amico & Montenbruck (JGCD 2006)** introduce the E/I-vector separation formulation of ROE that has driven every DLR/SLAB mission since. **Sullivan, Grimberg & D'Amico (JGCD 2017)** is the modern taxonomy of relative-motion models. **Koenig, Guffanti & D'Amico (JGCD 2017)** are the current state-of-the-art ROE STMs, extended with SRP by Guffanti & D'Amico (JGCD 2019) and with drag by Gaias, Ardaens & Montenbruck (CMDA 2015).
126	
127	### 2.2 Mission heritage that motivates the problem
128	
129	**PRISMA** (SSC/DLR/CNES, 2010): first European autonomous formation-flying demo, CDGPS relative nav, decimeter control at ~m/s/yr ΔV (D'Amico, Ardaens, Larsson, JGCD 2012). **TanDEM-X** (DLR, 2010): operational SAR interferometry pair, ~10 m 1σ autonomous control at ~5 m/s/yr ΔV via the TAFF onboard system using E/I-vector separation + relative-GPS filter (Montenbruck, Kahle, D'Amico, Ardaens, JAS 2008; Ardaens et al. IJSSE 2014). **GRACE / GRACE-FO** (NASA/GFZ, 2002/2018): mm-level baseline determination via CDGPS + K-band ranging (Kornfeld et al. JSR 2019; Kroes et al. GPS Solutions 2005). **MMS** (NASA, 2015): magnetospheric tetrahedron with GA-STM formation design (Roscoe, Vadali, Alfriend, Desai, JGCD 2011). **Proba-3** (ESA, launched Dec 2024, formation commissioning 2025): first millimeter/arcsecond formation in HEO. **NASA Starling** (four 6U CubeSats, launched July 2023): first on-orbit multi-observer angles-only relative navigation demonstration (Kruger, Koenig, D'Amico, JGCD 2025), first autonomous swarm station-keeping demonstration in November 2024 — with the observation from the flight-results paper that **full autonomous swarm maneuvering under closed-loop nav + control remains an open goal**. **CanX-4/5** (UTIAS-SFL, 2014): first nanosat FF demo, sub-meter control at ~1.5 m/s/yr ΔV (Kahr, Roth, Montenbruck, Risi, Zee, JSR 2018). **VISORS** (Stanford + CU + GT + others, launching soon): two-CubeSat solar-telescope formation targeting 15 mm precision at 40 m separation, with a CDGPS + consider-EKF pipeline (Guffanti, Bell, Low, Murray-Cooper, D'Amico, AAS 2023, arXiv 2309.16698).
130	
131	### 2.3 Relative navigation and Kalman filtering literature
132	
133	Carrier-phase differential GPS for formation flying begins with Busse, How & Simpson (Navigation 2003) — adaptive EKF; Kroes PhD Diss. (TU Delft 2006) — the standard spaceborne CDGPS reference; and Montenbruck, Wermuth & Kahle (Navigation 2011) for TanDEM-X flight results. Angles-only relative navigation has Woffinden (PhD 2008) and Woffinden & Geller (IEEE TAES 2009, JGCD 2009) for the observability criteria and optimal maneuvering; D'Amico et al. (JGCD 2013) for the ARGON flight experiment on PRISMA; Gaias, D'Amico & Ardaens (JGCD 2014) for ROE-based angles-only nav under J2; Sullivan & D'Amico (JGCD 2017) for nonlinear KF-in-ROE; Sullivan, Koenig, Kruger & D'Amico (JGCD 2021) for the generalized architecture; Willis & D'Amico (Adv. Space Res. 2024) for polynomial-dynamics acceleration. **These are the filters we will re-implement, calibrate against, and drive with.**
134	
135	### 2.4 The nav-versus-control literature — and its single most-cited pillar
136	
137	Exhaustive search across arXiv, JGCD, JAS, IEEE TAES, Acta Astronautica and mission-paper venues locates one paper that explicitly relates relative-nav error to J2 formation-keeping ΔV in a systematic way:
138	
139	> **Koenig, A. W., and D'Amico, S., "Relative Sensing, Control Precision, and Mission Delta-V Trade-Offs for Precision Formation Flying in Planetary Orbit," *Journal of Guidance, Control, and Dynamics* 41(5), 2018, pp. 1013–1025. DOI: 10.2514/1.G003316.**
140	
141	This is our closest antecedent and the paper we will explicitly extend. Its scope: an LTI analysis on a near-circular differential-J2 plant with an LQR controller and estimation error modeled as a static covariance floor; verification in a high-fidelity simulation; scaling relations from which a designer can read off "given σ_nav, expect Δv ~ f(σ_nav)". **Its limitations, which define our gap**: (i) linear time-invariant, i.e. near-circular chief only; (ii) LQR only — no impulsive Chernick–D'Amico, no MPC, no Lyapunov-δœ; (iii) estimator represented by a static covariance rather than by a full closed-loop nav+control simulation with an actual EKF/UKF; (iv) J2 enters the plant through the differential secular drift term but is not swept as a design axis; (v) the output is a single trade-off curve rather than a controller-and-STM-conditional Pareto surface.
142	
143	Adjacent recent work occupies the *cislunar* analog of the same problem: Bandyopadhyay et al. long-duration station-keeping strategy paper (arXiv 2507.19620, 2025) reports empirically that "absolute state knowledge error is the primary driver of ΔV consumption" for cislunar formations, and Hunter/Manuel/D'Amico (arXiv 2507.21425, 2025) present the first cislunar reachable-set solver + MPC to reject nav/dynamic errors. Covariance-steering / chance-constrained SCP methods (Ridderhof & Tsiotras 2021, Rapakoulias 2024, Kumagai & Oguri 2025) are mature in low-thrust powered descent and cislunar trajectory optimization but have **not been specialized to LEO J2 formation-keeping**. On the machine-learning side, differentiable-EKF-through-diffrax approaches have appeared in quadrotor control (arXiv 2508.21065, 2025) but not in spacecraft relative dynamics. Guffanti et al. VISORS paper (AAS 2023, arXiv 2309.16698) presents closed-loop MPC + CDGPS Monte-Carlo campaigns with ΔV histograms — but as an operational design study, not as a Pareto characterization.
144	
145	### 2.5 Novelty gap map
146	
147	The concrete gaps, ranked for a compact computational preprint, are:
148	
149	1. **A Pareto characterization of J2 formation-keeping ΔV as a function of (relative-nav 1σ, control precision) for LEO, spanning three controllers (steady-state LQR, receding-horizon MPC, closed-form impulsive Chernick–D'Amico) and three STMs (S-S, GA, KGD), driven by three realistic measurement architectures (CDGPS, RF range, angles-only ROE-EKF).** This is the direct generalization of Koenig & D'Amico 2018 along the axes it holds fixed. It is the paper.
150	2. **An STM-fidelity ablation that answers "for a fixed ΔV budget, is it cheaper to buy more model fidelity or more nav accuracy?"** — a corollary study made possible by (1). Nothing published answers this directly.
151	3. A negative or nuanced result on the certainty-equivalence assumption under J2: identify the nav-error regime in which u = −K X̂ measurably underperforms a nav-aware controller, quantifying the underperformance.
152	
153	Gaps 4–6 (covariance-steering formulation for LEO/J2, observability-aware maneuvering with cost budgets, differentiable-EKF-through-diffrax policy learning) are attractive but each carries larger scope or larger scoop risk than the compressed timeline can absorb. We defer them to follow-on work and mention them explicitly in the paper's Discussion section.
154	
155	**Defensibility of gap #1.** It is (a) computationally tractable in weeks, not months; (b) a direct extension of a single well-known prior paper — the reviewer's first question is always "how does this differ from Koenig & D'Amico 2018?" and we have a five-item answer; (c) genuinely useful to mission designers (Starling successors, VISORS follow-ons, distributed-aperture concepts); (d) undone in the LEO/J2 setting despite the parallel 2025–2026 cislunar work implicitly pointing toward it.
156	
157	---
158	
159	## 3. Methodology and Python toolchain
160	
161	### 3.1 Recommended primary stack
162	
163	The tooling reflects the current (mid-2026) state of the Python astrodynamics ecosystem. Key facts driving the recommendation: **poliastro is archived** (Oct 2023) and **hapsira, its community fork, has effectively bus-factor one** with the last PyPI release in Dec 2023 — neither is defensible as a research-critical dependency. **Orekit's JCC-based wrapper (`orekit_python`) is being retired in favor of `orekit-jpype`**, whose current release is 13.1.4.2 (April 2026). **Basilisk 2.10.x** ships as pip-installable wheels since v2.9, is permissively ISC-licensed, and has formation-flying as a first-class citizen. **Nyx's Python bindings are temporarily disabled** in the 2.0 cycle, and its core is AGPL-3.0, contagious to derivative code — avoid.
164	
165	The recommended primary stack:
166	
167	| Layer | Choice | Rationale |
168	|---|---|---|
169	| Frames, times, units | astropy | Ubiquitous, tested, permissive |
170	| TLE ingestion (initial conditions) | sgp4 (Rhodes) | Reference implementation |
171	| High-fidelity truth propagator | **Basilisk 2.10.x** | ISC license, pip-installable, formation-flying native, bit-for-bit-repeatable Monte-Carlo engine, Docker images published |
172	| Independent cross-validator | **orekit-jpype 13.1.x** | Best-in-class spherical harmonics, IERS, atmospheric models; Apache-2.0; publish a table showing Basilisk vs. Orekit agreement to <10 m over the campaign |
173	| Analytic relative-motion models (CW, S-S, GA-STM, KGD-STM) | Hand-rolled NumPy modules | Small (~500 LOC each), fully testable, publishable as the paper's core contribution alongside the analysis |
174	| Integrator for analytic-model IVPs | scipy.solve_ivp with DOP853 | Standard, high-order, stable |
175	| Filter | Hand-rolled EKF and UKF in NumPy (Joseph form; Merwe sigma points for UKF); filterpy for cross-checking on toy problems | filterpy has not released since 2018 — fine for reference, not for building on |
176	| Controllers | python-control (`lqr`, `care`) for LQR; hand-rolled Chernick–D'Amico closed-form for impulsive; CasADi 3.7.x + IPOPT for MPC | All permissively licensed |
177	| Monte Carlo | Basilisk's native MC + joblib for envelope-level parallelism; JAX + diffrax as an optional accelerator if the campaign becomes too large | diffrax 0.7.2+ is stable |
178	| Reproducibility | uv for env, Docker for hardened builds, GitHub Actions for CI, Zenodo for the DOI | uv is now the community consensus |
179	
180	### 3.2 Why Basilisk over Orekit as the *primary* truth propagator
181	
182	Orekit is the more mature astrodynamics kernel — better spherical harmonics, IERS conventions, atmospheric models, GNSS support. Basilisk is not as deep in raw astrodynamics, but it is **designed** for exactly this workload: multi-spacecraft, message-passing state exchange, sensor/actuator models, bit-for-bit Monte Carlo. For a J2 formation-flying paper where truth up to J4/J6 is sufficient, Basilisk gets us to a working formation simulation faster and reproduces more cleanly in a container. **Orekit-jpype runs in parallel as the independent cross-validator**; publishing a table showing agreement between the two truth propagators to <10 m over the campaign is exactly the validation pattern reviewers expect.
183	
184	### 3.3 Validation strategy
185	
186	Each analytic relative-motion model (CW, S-S, GA-STM, KGD-STM) is validated by simulating the same initial condition in the Basilisk high-fidelity truth propagator (J2 through J4, no drag/SRP in the primary sweep to isolate the effect being studied) and computing per-orbit RMS Cartesian error of the analytic prediction against the truth trajectory over 30 days. Expected order-of-magnitude behavior: CW diverges quickly (meters per orbit for a 1-km LEO formation); S-S holds to tens of centimeters per orbit under its assumptions; GA-STM and KGD-STM hold to centimeters. This validation table is Figure 2 of the paper.
187	
188	### 3.4 Monte-Carlo campaign structure
189	
190	A single **campaign point** is a (controller, STM, measurement architecture, σ_nav, geometry) tuple, run over N Monte-Carlo trials with different random seeds for measurement noise and process noise. For each trial we record: the LVLH trajectory, the nav filter's estimated trajectory, the applied control commands, per-orbit ΔV consumption, RMS formation-keeping error, filter innovation statistics, and end-of-campaign delta-œ. The full study is a **grid sweep**:
191	
192	- 3 controllers × 3 STMs × 3 measurement architectures × ~6 σ_nav points × ~4 geometries = ~648 campaign points
193	- N = 100 seeds per point ⇒ ~65,000 total simulations
194	- Each simulation is ~1 minute wall-clock on a modern laptop core (30-day campaign, 60 s propagation step); ~1,100 core-hours total
195	- Feasible on a single 32-core workstation in ~1.5 days or on cloud burst in ~4 hours
196	
197	We reduce cost with a two-tier strategy: a low-fidelity screening pass with S-S plants for all points to identify the "interesting" regions, then full high-fidelity truth-propagator runs only for the boundary points.
198	
199	### 3.5 Reproducibility checklist
200	
201	- `uv` for dependency resolution; both `pyproject.toml` and `uv.lock` committed
202	- Docker container based on `python:3.12-slim` pinned by digest; Basilisk installed from the official multi-arch image; Orekit-jpype via pip with the `[jdk4py]` extra
203	- Master `numpy.random.SeedSequence` in `config/seeds.yaml`; per-trial child seeds recorded in the output Parquet
204	- Figure regeneration via a `Snakefile` DAG: `configs → simulations → parquet → figures`; every figure regenerable from `snakemake figures`
205	- CI matrix on GitHub Actions across Python 3.11/3.12/3.13 × {Linux, macOS}: runs unit tests, N=32 mini-campaign, hashes a canonical figure and diffs against a committed reference
206	- Zenodo integration on the GitHub repo; the arXiv-submission tag mints both a specific-version DOI and a concept DOI; the CITATION.cff renders GitHub's "Cite this repository" button
207	- License: MIT for the paper's code (permissively compatible with every upstream dependency in the primary stack)
208
